pr:section.pr:title: Schema Design Decisions
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Key choices in the XSD schemas and their rationale.
pr:section.pr:p: Individual layer schemas handle structural validation: element shapes, attribute types, enumerations. The combined schema handles referential validation: ensuring all cross-layer references resolve.

pr:ul.pr:li: xs:string for cross-layer reference attributes (not xs:IDREF, which is single-document scoped).
xs:unique for the unified ID space (not xs:key, which requires every matched element to have the field).

Explicit element refs in the generated combined schema (not xs:any, which breaks keyref enforcement in the xmlschema library). Each layer schema marks its content elements with spec:content-element appinfo annotations, and the generator collects these into the choice group.
The spec:clayers root element with xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict" for open-world extensibility.
Every layer root element is spec:clayers, distinguished only by the namespaces of its children. Layer schemas are element libraries: they define global elements and types but no root element.

pr:section.pr:p: Cross-layer keyrefs are declared in individual layer schemas via spec:keyref appinfo annotations, but are emitted in the generated combined schema. xs:keyref requires its referenced xs:unique to be in scope (same element or ancestor). In standalone layer files, target IDs from other layers are not present, so the keyrefs can only be enforced in the combined document.

pr:section.pr:title: Open-World Layer Model
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How xs:any enables adding layers and mixing content without modifying existing schemas.
pr:section.pr:p: The layer system follows an open-world model. The spec schema (spec.xsd) defines a single clayers root element with xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict" for its content model. This means any element from any namespace (other than the spec namespace itself) is accepted as a child, and each child is validated against its own namespace's schema.

This design enables two key capabilities. First, new layers can be added by creating a new schema with global element declarations, without modifying spec.xsd or any existing layer schema. Second, a single file can contain elements from multiple layers, enabling cross-layer mixing at the file level.

The combined schema for cross-layer validation is generated dynamically. Each layer schema annotates its content elements with spec:content-element in appinfo, and declares its cross-layer keyrefs with spec:keyref annotations. The validator scans all .xsd files, collects these annotations, and generates the combined schema at validation time. Adding a new layer requires only the layer schema itself: no central schema file needs editing.
pr:section.pr:note: All files share the same root element name (spec:clayers). The namespace of the children is the sole discriminator for content type. A file with pr:section children is a prose file, one with idx:file children is an index, etc. This makes the format instantly recognizable: any file starting with clayers is a clayers file.

pr:section.pr:title: Index and Spec Identity
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the index manifest defines a spec's boundary, identity, and file structure.
pr:section.pr:p: The index is the entry point for a specification. Its root element carries two identity attributes: spec (a unique identifier for the specification) and version (the index snapshot version).

The index lists all layer files via file elements, each with an href (relative path) and an optional layer (namespace URI identifying the primary layer type). This manifest defines the spec boundary: only files listed here participate in combined-document assembly and cross-layer validation.

The index attribute on the clayers root element serves as the back-reference. Each non-index layer declares its index, making every file self-describing: given any layer file, the validator can resolve which spec it belongs to without relying on directory structure or naming conventions.

pr:section.pr:title: Revisions and Temporal Anchoring
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How named snapshots anchor artifact mappings to specific points in a spec's evolution.
pr:section.pr:p: Each revision records a named snapshot: a name (such as "draft-1" or "v1.0"), a timestamp, a reference to the index file, and a hash of that index. The name is the stable handle that artifact mappings use to pin their spec-refs to a specific point in time.

The revision layer uses a spec attribute on its root element rather than the index back-reference used by other layers. This is because revisions identify the specification by name, not by file path: the same spec identity persists even if the index file is renamed or moved.

When the tooling needs to determine the "current" revision (for example, when deciding which artifact hashes to update), it selects the revision with the latest timestamp. Spec-refs pinned to older revisions are left unchanged: their hashes are historically correct for the spec content at that point in time.

pr:section.pr:title: Extensible Relation Types
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How extension layers define their own relation types without modifying relation.xsd.
pr:section.pr:p: The RelationKind enumeration in relation.xsd is a closed set. Extension layers cannot add values to it without modifying the core schema. Extensible relation types solve this by turning XSD types into graph nodes and using the spec's own relation graph for discovery.
The mechanism has three parts:

pr:ol.pr:li: Addressable schema types. XSD types carry id attributes (e.g., id="type-relation-kind" on the RelationKind simpleType), making them nodes in the spec graph alongside content nodes. The tooling scans all .xsd files for elements with @id attributes and merges them into the graph.

Extension type annotation. An extension layer defines its own XSD element and complexType in its own namespace. The complexType carries a rel:relation-type-properties annotation in xs:appinfo, declaring: the canonical relation-name for graph edges and reports, whether the type is acyclic (must form a DAG), and which attributes hold the source and target node IDs (from-attr and to-attr, defaulting to "from" and "to").

Activation via refines edge. A rel:relation type="refines" edge from the extension type's @id to type-relation-kind declares the extension as a relation type. The tooling follows these edges to discover which annotated types are active.

pr:section.pr:p: For example, a traceability extension layer might define:
pr:section.pr:codeblock: <xs:complexType name="TraceLinkType" id="type-trace-link"> <xs:annotation><xs:appinfo> <rel:relation-type-properties acyclic="true" relation-name="trace-link" from-attr="source" to-attr="target"/> </xs:appinfo></xs:annotation> <xs:attribute name="source" type="xs:string" use="required"/> <xs:attribute name="target" type="xs:string" use="required"/> </xs:complexType>
pr:section.pr:p: With a refines edge in spec content: <rel:relation type="refines" from="type-trace-link" to="type-relation-kind"/>

The connectivity tool uses a two-pass collection process to resolve the chicken-and-egg problem (extension types are discovered from refines edges, but those edges are themselves relations). Pass 1 collects all explicit rel:relation elements, including the refines edges that activate extensions. Pass 2 scans the combined document for instances of the discovered extension elements, using the declared attribute names to extract source and target node IDs.
pr:section.pr:note: Extension types use domain-specific attribute names (like source/target) instead of the generic from/to. The from-attr/to-attr properties in the annotation tell the tooling which attributes to read.

pr:section.pr:title: Schema Directory Discovery
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the tooling locates schema files automatically by walking up from the working directory.
pr:section.pr:p: Several commands need access to the layer schemas: the schema command exports them as RNC, and the connectivity command reads them to discover acyclic relation type annotations. Rather than requiring an explicit schema path every time, the tooling auto-discovers the schema directory.

Discovery walks up the directory tree from a starting point (the spec directory for connectivity, the current working directory for the schema command). At each level it checks two locations in order:

pr:ol.pr:li: schemas/ — the canonical location in the source repository (used during development).
.clayers/schemas/ — the adopted location planted by clayers adopt (used in downstream projects).

pr:section.pr:p: The first directory found wins. This means a project that has both locations (e.g., the clayers repo after self-adoption) will prefer schemas/, preserving the source-of-truth behavior for schema development. Downstream projects that only have the adopted schemas will find .clayers/schemas/ automatically.
The schema command's path argument becomes optional: when omitted, it uses auto-discovery from the current directory. When provided, it bypasses discovery entirely.

org:reference.org:purpose: Rationale for key XSD design decisions, useful when extending or debugging schemas.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the type hierarchy enables extensibility without modifying existing layer schemas.
Understanding how the index defines spec identity, boundary, and file structure.
Understanding how revisions provide temporal anchoring for artifact mappings and how the tooling determines the current revision.
Understanding how the tooling locates schema files automatically, enabling adopted projects to use clayers commands without explicit schema paths.
Understanding how extension layers define custom relation types without modifying the core relation schema, and how the tooling discovers and processes them via the spec's own relation graph.

rel:relation.rel:note: Schema design decisions are the XSD implementation of the cross-layer integrity concept.
The open-world model explains how the layered architecture achieves extensibility via xs:any in spec.xsd.
The xs:any namespace="##other" pattern is a key schema design decision that enables open-world extensibility and mixing.
The revision layer details temporal anchoring, a key property of the layered architecture.
The index layer details spec identity and boundary, the foundation of the layered architecture.
Extensible relation types extend the open-world model from structural layers (adding new element namespaces) to semantic types (adding new relation kinds via annotation and graph discovery).
Extensible relation types depend on the connectivity analysis tool to discover and process extension types via the two-pass collection.
Extension types can declare acyclicity, which feeds into the cycle detection mechanism.
Schema discovery finds .clayers/schemas/ directories that are planted by the adopt command.
Schema discovery is the runtime mechanism that connects the open-world layer model to adopted projects by locating their planted schemas.
XSD design choices (xs:unique vs xs:key, explicit refs vs xs:any) directly constrain how the validator can operate.

trm:term.trm:name: RelationType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of relation elements. Extension relation types extend this type.

trm:term.trm:name: RelationKind
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD simpleType that enumerates recognized relation type values. Extension relation types add values via a refines edge to this type.

rel:relation.rel:note: The RelationType complexType (id="type-relation") is the schema definition that extensible relation types extend.
The RelationKind simpleType (id="type-relation-kind") is the activation target for extension relation types.

art:mapping.art:note: Acyclic type discovery and collection: discover_acyclic_types(), collect_acyclic_types(), has_acyclic_true().
Content element discovery from XSD: discover_content_elements(), discover_from_xsd() scanning appinfo annotations.
Content element collection and schema output generation: collect_content_elements(), has_content_element_annotation().
spec.xsd defines the clayers root element with xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict", enabling the open-world layer model and cross-layer mixing.
revision.xsd defines the revision structure: name, timestamp, index reference, and index-hash attributes.
index.xsd defines the file manifest element and spec identity. The index attribute back-reference is now in spec.xsd.

RelationType id attribute (37), relation-type-properties element declaration (77-108), RelationKind id attribute (110). These three XSD additions make types addressable and provide the annotation mechanism.
collect_schema_nodes() (373-386), discover_extension_relation_types() (389-451), _collect_relations_extended() two-pass collection (454-519), connectivity command integration (1757-1761).

spec:clayers.llm:schema: The index schema defines the file manifest for a specification. The <file> element lists layer files via href (relative path) and an optional layer (namespace URI) attribute. The index establishes the ID uniqueness domain: all @id values across listed files must be unique.

A file reference within the index manifest. The href attribute is a relative path to the layer file. The optional layer attribute is the namespace URI identifying the primary layer type the file contains.

The revision schema records named snapshots of a specification at points in time. Each <revision> has a name (like "draft-1" or "v1.0"), a timestamp, a reference to the index file, and a hash of that index. Artifact mappings pin to revision names so that hashes can be anchored to a specific point in the spec's evolution.
spec:clayers.llm:node: Rationale for key XSD design decisions. Consult this when extending schemas or debugging validation errors. Covers: xs:unique vs xs:key, xs:string vs xs:IDREF, explicit refs vs xs:any, spec:content-element and spec:keyref annotations, and why keyrefs are emitted in the generated combined schema.

Explains the open-world layer model: spec.xsd defines a clayers root element with xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict", accepting children from any layer namespace. New layers can be added without modifying existing schemas. Files can mix elements from multiple layers. The combined schema is generated dynamically from layer schema annotations.

Describes the index as the spec entry point: spec/version identity attributes, file manifest with href+layer, and the index attribute on the clayers root that makes every layer file self-describing. Foundation for understanding spec discovery and validation.

Explains how extension layers define custom relation types. Three-part mechanism: (1) XSD types get id attributes making them graph nodes, (2) extension complexTypes carry rel:relation-type-properties annotations declaring relation-name, acyclicity, and from/to attribute names, (3) a refines edge from the extension type id to type-relation-kind activates the extension. The connectivity tool uses two-pass collection: pass 1 gathers explicit rel:relation elements (discovering refines edges), pass 2 scans for instances of activated extension elements. Extension types can use domain-specific attribute names (e.g. source/target instead of from/to) via from-attr/to-attr properties.

Explains revision semantics: named snapshots with timestamps, how artifact mappings pin to revision names, the spec attribute (not index), and how tooling picks the current revision by latest timestamp. Important context for understanding artifact hash maintenance.

art:mapping.art:note: find_schema_dir() walks up from a starting path checking schemas/ then .clayers/schemas/ at each level.
analyze_connectivity() uses find_schema_dir() to locate schemas for acyclic relation type discovery.
resolve_schema_dir() and cmd_schema() make the schema command's path argument optional via auto-discovery.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Schema directory discovery walks up the directory tree checking two locations at each level: schemas/ (canonical source location) and .clayers/schemas/ (adopted location). First match wins. This enables the schema and connectivity commands to work without explicit schema paths in both development repos (where schemas/ exists) and adopted projects (where .clayers/schemas/ was planted by clayers adopt). The schema command's path argument becomes optional when discovery is available.

art:mapping.art:note: RelationType complexType defines the structure of relation elements with from, to, type, acyclic attributes and note child.
RelationKind simpleType enumerates allowed relation types: depends-on, references, refines, constrains, and extension types via union.
